title: Nijolė's Cat
description:
When the artistic initiatives began in 2012, you could feel a real distance between residents — neighbors barely knew each other, and there was no living, direct connection between them. Constant litter and indifference to the living environment pushed us to look for ways to turn the courtyard into a place that wasn't just somewhere pleasant and interesting to live, but a space for connection — one where a new kind of social phenomenon could take shape: the dissolving of the distance between people.

"Nijolė's Cat," perched in the window with the yellow shutters, didn't appear by chance. Its blue eyes followed the neighbors taking out their trash every day. The cat's unusual gaze invited residents to see their surroundings with fresh eyes — perhaps to notice them at all, to stop and think: don't litter.

Portrait of "Neighbor Nijolė" (visualization project). The woman doesn't just love and tend flowers herself — she inspires other courtyard residents to decorate their own windows and balconies with flower arrangements.

title: Portrait of Neighbor Mrs. Kazimiera
description:
In 2018, a life-size painting of a woman with roses was created by the courtyard gate — a tribute to the Dapkevičius sisters, who lived in the courtyard in the postwar years and grew flowers.

On one of the buildings, there's a story told about sister Kazimiera, who once lived here and used to plant and tend flowers by the fountain in the courtyard, decorating the yard and bringing joy to everyone who lived here.

In 2015, during the "Nykoka" street art festival, glass artist and Kiemo Galerija co-author Ieva Voroneckytė, together with volunteers, helped plant the mirrored flowers.

There's no room to grow flowers here anymore, so all that's left is to remember, on the courtyard walls, the history and beauty that this woman created.

title: Portraits of Neighbors Saulius and Ritma
description:
Neighbors Saulius and his wife Ritma have also lived in our courtyard for a long time. Their son's approaching wedding brought back memories of the wedding rituals once held here, and inspired two artworks in their honor — "The Wedding Clothes Wardrobe" and "Saulius's Shoes." The real-life people behind the story helped bring both pieces to life — the "wardrobe" was filled with the bride's and groom's retro-style shoes, which were painted white and set into the wall painting.

For the mural of their own wedding photo, they were not only the subjects of the piece but also took part in creating it. On their son's wedding day, the whole family and their relatives had their picture taken by the artworks depicting their family history.

"Saulius's Shoes": at the unveiling of the "Holy Trinity" painting, Saulius drove in the nail himself and we "hung" them there. Those shoes had sat by the door for a long time, so I asked if I could paint them.

"It's as if I'd never seen shoes before — I start studying their look, their expression... their shape, the worn-down uppers, and I notice that the creases and white stitching give them a kind of expression, a physiognomy. Something of my own being passed into those shoes; they gave off a sense of closeness, a living part of myself..."

title: Imagine
description:
"Imagine" is an intertextual site-specific artwork drawing on two works: John Lennon's song "Imagine," which has inspired peace activists worldwide for nearly four decades, and Rembrandt's painting "The Return of the Prodigal Son," which depicts the biblical story of a son who squandered his wealth and returned home to his father asking for forgiveness. This reinterpretation depicts the son at the very start of his life's journey — a curious boy.

Rembrandt's painting became the starting point for exploring the theme of returning home: to return, first of all, you have to remember where you came from.

"All grown-ups were once children, although few of them remember it." — Antoine de Saint-Exupéry, The Little Prince

title: Safe Neighborhood
description:
Kiemo Galerija has been a member of the "Safe Neighborhood" movement for several years now — announced by the yellow signs on the walls and the security cameras installed around the courtyard. The concept of a "safe neighborhood" isn't always understood the same way by residents, and it often becomes a topic of discussion...

So the idea arose to create a wry, good-humored piece depicting three neighborhood women sitting on a bench, knitting, shelling sunflower seeds, watching the people and the world around them — a nod to a bygone era when there were no cameras or fences, and the neighbor women knew everything about everyone, and then some.

This group portrait of the neighbors reminds us that thirty years ago there was a bench in the courtyard where the women would often sit, knit, watch the world go by, and chat about life.

title: Black House
description:
The first phase of the courtyard's set design — the Black House — was carried out in 2018. By deliberately using the contrast of achromatic colors and the layered-plaster imitation typical of the sgraffito technique, a colorful, relief pattern resembling a floral knit was drawn onto the building's façade. The Black House project set out not only to show the neighbors' pastime of knitting and crochet, but also to draw attention to the color palette of a building that had never been painted — a palette only revealed once a black base coat was applied.

Slide for transforming the Black House:

An important detail: Kiemo Galerija holds to the core principle that the "old colors of the buildings" are part of our courtyard's historical heritage, and it's only by introducing other, additional colors that the old colors — like a layer of the past — come alive again and are seen anew... I play with the cultural heritage, I try to preserve it authentically, I negotiate with builders for whom a freshly plastered and painted wall is the "correct" one. I'm glad when I manage to convince them that a wall is more beautiful with the marks of time on it, especially in a courtyard like ours, which is more than 130 years old.

Creators of the Black House's knit pattern: ceramic artist Tomas Zlotnikovas and my son Edvardas. 2018.

title: Dream Sewing Workshop
description: In the same year the Black House was created, Kiemo Galerija was graced by my daughter's creative project — artist Staselė Jakunskaitė's "Dream Sewing Workshop." Its vividly colorful palette inspired us to look at the other buildings, whose walls hadn't been painted in years, in a more colorful light too.

title: Portrait of Artist Zan Hoffman
description:
Project visualization

"There is no box. Which means there are no rules to break, no limitations. I don't have to do anything conventional to be recognized as an artist. I don't have to create anything that would get me into a museum. I create what is valuable in itself, or what feels important to me."

Zan Hoffman, an artist and showman from Louisville, Kentucky, comes to our Kiemo Galerija celebration. He's the mind behind three music projects and proudly owns a tie collection of more than 900 pieces.

title: Portrait of Petriukas
description:
Visualization project

The oldest photograph in neighbor Regina's family album, one recalling the interwar years, was found — Regina's father as a child.

Regina told us she spent more time with her father, Petras, than with her mother, and still thinks of him often even now, in her own old age. The photo chosen for the courtyard portrait is one she found in the family album while going through his things after his funeral.

The portrait of Regina's father will be rendered in a monochrome painting technique.

title: Portrait of Eliza Ožeškienė
description:
The old information board with a changed address: (Stalino Ave. / Ožeškienės St. No. 15)

This considerably rusted board hung empty; our names appeared on it after a neighbors' dinner. During the interwar period, once the Stalinist regime began, boards like this could be found in almost every larger courtyard.

These information boards were put up to make it easier to find residents who were being sought. During the repressions, the aim was to quickly locate people who were to be arrested and deported. But as people began disappearing more and more often, these boards stopped helping and instead started exposing the regime's own activity. So the boards were banned and ordered taken down — but here, in our courtyard, this one survived.

The old information board still reminds us of who Stalin and his regime were, yet most people from Kaunas — and most tourists — don't know who Ožeškienė was, or why her name was given to this street.

Many people from Kaunas will find it interesting to learn that the street was named after her in 1919, replacing what had been Kurovska Street (information from M. Balkus's publication "Kaunas Street Names 1918–1940" in the Kaunas History Yearbook).

The author notes that "as the history of Kaunas shows, it was rare for a city street to keep its name from when it was first laid out. In the 20th century, most streets in central Kaunas changed their names at least three or four times — nearly every time the country's political system changed."

So who was this Eliza Ožeškienė?

Eliza Paulauskaitė-Ožeškienė-Nagorskienė (Polish: Eliza Pawłowska-Orzeszkowa-Nahorska, June 6, 1841 – May 18, 1910) was a writer, novelist, critic, essayist, and public figure. She was born in what is now Belarus, lived for a long time in Liudvinavas and Grodno, and often stayed in Druskininkai, Panemunė, and Vilnius. She published her work under the pen names Gabriela Litwinka and Li...ka (Lika).

While representing Polish culture, Eliza Ožeškienė also felt a deep attachment to her native land. Lithuania was her homeland — the nation she felt part of lived here, even though she spoke and wrote in a language other than Lithuanian. She didn't tie the language she used in everyday life and in her work to national identity, which she understood instead in terms of the region where she was born and lived — the former lands of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania. She actively took part in the 1863 uprising: carrying mail and food to the insurgents, nursing the wounded, and hiding the uprising's leader, Romuald Traugutt, on her estate...

"I know for certain that I have never visited this city, but I feel... that I have already been here."

This project presents a mural — an artwork built around the phenomenon of déjà vu — depicting a portrait of Eliza Ožeškienė, a woman who, as far as we know, never actually visited Kaunas.

The project's concept rests on the principles of site-specific art: on one hand, a piece of street art draws attention to places people no longer notice — in this case, a street name very familiar to both residents and visitors of Kaunas; on the other, it raises the question of who this woman actually was, whose name, it turns out, has been given to this Kaunas street for more than a hundred years.

The portrait of Eliza Ožeškienė was created in 2024. The piece was supported by Kauno Akcentai.

title: Blue House
description:
In 2020, during the international project "Wall Art for Communities," together with neighbor Saulė, we carried out the second phase of the courtyard's set design — the Blue House.

The Blue House's design deliberately used a mirror mosaic that reflects the climbing vines growing on the building opposite, filling the courtyard not only with blue but with the colors of the building across from it too.

Blue symbolizes peace and calm — think of the stillness we feel lying on the ground and looking up at a clear sky. The lighter the blue, the freer we feel. It's said that blue is people's favorite color of all; psychologists suspect this is because of the sense of security it gives.

title: The Garden
description:
Because the courtyard's parking area is so cramped, there's now no room to grow not just flowers, but even trees. How do you solve that dilemma and turn "little" into "much"? What can't be solved physically can perhaps be attempted metaphysically — using the metaphor of a "Straw Garden"...

So this year, at Kiemo Galerija, we're planning to build a 4.5-meter-diameter garden installation out of weather-resistant synthetic materials, echoing the mirrored, triangle-built structure of the White House.

"These geometric shapes weren't chosen by accident. In ancient cultures, triangles symbolized spirit, and squares symbolized matter. It's the harmony of the two that makes up the garden."

It's likely that the slowly turning garden will not only cleanse the surrounding homes energetically, but will also send out vibrations of harmony.

"Standing in its presence, you'll find stillness and a quiet joy, as if glimpsing a perfect image of the Universe."

Artists: Justas and Gintarė Ivanauskas, Aurelija Kurienė, Edvardas Jakunskas, and Beatriz Oliveira helped bring the Garden installation to life.

September 23, 2023

title: Rooms of Secrets
description:
"Everything we see hides something else; we always want to see what is behind, but that is impossible. People guard their secrets very carefully..." (R. Magritte)

The premise behind the "Rooms of Secrets" project was shaped in part by some of the ideas of artist R. Magritte — namely, the concept of secrecy. According to the artist, the world holds some secret hidden from ordinary human eyes; art itself is a secret... This becomes relevant when talking about relationships too, including between neighbors. Sometimes we deliberately close ourselves off from the people living beside us; other times we seek connection, togetherness, the sharing of secrets and the desire to know one another.

What is a secret? Why does this idea of secrecy matter in our courtyard? Our courtyard is visited not only by individual tourists but by large groups — people of different cultures, perspectives, and life experiences...

While looking at the artworks, visitors often glance up at the windows of the people who currently live here, and sometimes wander into a stairwell, curiously knocking on doors... Seeing the open, exposed exterior — the courtyard — they want to know the interior too, to learn more. To uncover the secrets of this shared existence? A great many of them are kept by the walls of these homes, which remember the twists and turns of different eras. The current residents have their own secrets too — we don't know everything about each other, and sometimes we share secrets when we meet in the courtyard... and perhaps we don't know everything about ourselves either — our shared existence remains, to us, a secret.

In "Rooms of Secrets," the meaning of this confrontation with secrecy is built through a principle of reconciling contradictions — depicted as three white and three black rooms, arranged like a chessboard across three floors. The common axis running through all the rooms is the secret itself, which the viewers of the piece construct for themselves.

(Texts for "Rooms of Secrets" written by Prof. Saulutė Juzelėnienė)

Click on each room to learn more about the human stories within it.

Fifth Room

Sixth Room

Third Room

Fourth Room

First Room

Second Room

The overall atmosphere of "Rooms of Secrets" is created by intersecting lines marked out in small mirror fragments, tracing the principle of perspective.

Sometimes even a small dot on a wall can become a meaningful work of art. Here, the concept of perspective used by Antique and Renaissance artists can be extended in many directions, encompassing not only linear perspective but the broader perspectives of human connection. Today, contemporary artists share new insights as the world stands at an existential threshold — which direction should we choose, which perspective is the right one? In this sense, art, as a tremendous creative force, could become a means of encouraging human connection and awareness.

title: White House
description:
The first phase of the White House's set design began in 2014. During the "Nykoka" street art festival, a painting of the "Holy Trinity" was created on the building's east-facing wall at Kiemo Galerija, bearing the inscription "In my Father's house there are many rooms." And indeed, wherever you look around the courtyard, there are windows upon windows, dwellings upon dwellings. In their abundance, Christ's words take on flesh: we are all connected, and the unity that binds us — unchanging across the ages — is an invitation to be genuine, conscious people. This meaningful inscription is dedicated specifically to the courtyard's residents, marking the importance of togetherness.

The first mirrored flowers, symbolizing the three sisters who once lived here, decorated the walls back at the 2014 "Nykoka" street art festival. The theme for the "Create Your Life's Flower" workshop was set by one of the sisters, Kazimiera, who used to decorate the little courtyard with flowers.

In the summer of 2015, together with neighbors, we freed a bathtub someone had abandoned amid the rubble. This sight has caught the attention of more than a few people from Kaunas — it's clearly visible not only from within Kiemo Galerija itself, but also from up on Savanorių Hill as you come down.

Above the rubble, the building debris and the litter, a site-specific artwork was installed together with the neighbors on a ledge of the red-brick façade — inviting reflection not only on the relationship between people and objects, on what objects are for and how they become trash, but also, metaphorically, on the estrangement between people.

The "Small Walls Project" and Kiemo Galerija, together with artists Timotiejus Norvila ("Morfai") and Tadas Vincaitis ("Plugas"), realized the vision of the "Stork" in 2017...

"Lithuanians have long loved, cared for, and even revered storks; they're seen as bringers of good fortune. Between the stork and humankind there has long existed something like a spiritual bond, a mutual draw. Much of the stork's popularity comes down to its remarkable inclination to draw near to people and trust them... To this day, many a Lithuanian homestead owner works hard to nurture this friendship, since it's not just a decoration for the homestead but a symbol of family harmony and fidelity. Storks are humankind's faithful companions."

The Stork didn't land at Kiemo Galerija by accident. Symbolizing longevity in Eastern culture, and immortality in Daoism, this winged visitor to the heart of Ožeškienės Street calls to mind not only questions of migration but of the birth rate too. Notably, at the time the Stork vision was being created, there were no children living in the courtyard.

In July 2021, Kiemo Galerija held an open creative workshop during which volunteers from "Kaunas 2022"'s community program "Fluxus Labas!" could create their own life's flower on the walls of the White House using pieces of mirror. Mirrors are the White House's central motif. I chose mirrors for this piece because a mirror is the reflection that exists closest to the reality of the here and now — it simply reflects the PRESENT. Each of us starts our morning with a mirror — a here-and-now reflection with our surroundings and with ourselves...

In 2023, on the White House's ground floor, together with neighbors and artists, we extended sister Kazimiera's mirrored flower composition. Following the visible cut-paper knit motif, we created a mirrored flower garden around the White House's east, south, and north walls. Unlike the Black House, where the knit pattern was done in the sgraffito technique, here on the White House it took on the character of a sculptural bas-relief.

title: Jokūbas Grinbergas's Houses
description:
In 2024, funding provided by Kauno Akcentai made it possible to continue and complete the set-design project for Jokūbas Grinbergas's Houses.

The central motif of Jokūbas Grinbergas's Houses is the color blue, colored glass, and mirrors. These materials were chosen for their durability and their reflective qualities.

The interior façade patterns and decorative window elements are cut from foundation foam board and filled in with mirror mosaics. This technique is used throughout Kiemo Galerija. Wherever you turn, the eye is drawn to colorful mirrored reflections.

title: Children's Action
description:
During the Great Action, selected Jews from the ghetto are marched to Kaunas's Ninth Fort to be shot (from E. Oshry's book The Annihilation of Lithuanian Jewry, 1995).

"On one of the most tragic days in the history of the Kaunas ghetto — March 27, 1944 — one of the most brutal crimes of the Nazi regime was carried out: the Children's Action. That day, children under the age of 12 were torn from their mothers' arms and taken from the Kaunas ghetto to Auschwitz. Early that same morning, 140 Jewish ghetto policemen were arrested and taken to the Ninth Fort, where 33 of them were murdered. Among those killed — brutally tortured, yet never revealing the hiding places where children and the ghetto resistance's weapons were concealed — were deputy chief of the Jewish police Juda (Jehuda) Zupavičius and policeman Ika Grinbergas."

title: Portrait of Ika Grinberg (1)
description: "Here, from 1920 to 1941, lived Ika Grinbergas. As a Kaunas ghetto policeman, he took an active part in the underground resistance. Without betraying anyone, he was shot by the Nazis at the Ninth Fort."

title: Portrait of Ika Grinberg (2)
description:
In one of the "Rooms of Secrets," the shadow of a young woman gazing at a mirrored window reflecting the clouds and sky guards the secret of her love, still unaware of so much to come... In Dita's room, we travel back to the interwar years, when a young Jewish girl, Dita, in love with her neighbor Ika, would turn off the light in the evenings and look toward the window of the building across from hers, hoping to catch a glimpse of him... Past and present, a love stronger than the death that the Holocaust used to separate these eternal sweethearts forever — this shaped not only the portrait of Ika Grinbergas, son of Jokūbas Grinbergas, but also the mood of the set design for the buildings where they lived.

On the other side, on the wall of the building where he lived, you see a portrait of Ika Grinbergas as a young secondary-school student — an artist, and later an officer in the interwar Lithuanian army. Feelings and memories of youth, still touching a woman's heart, shaped the composition of this artwork — which is why I wanted to paint the two of them facing each other, even if only in a small-format mural, joining their souls together.

title: Portrait of Dita and Juda Zupavičius
description:
In the summer of 2014, Dita Šperling (Zupavičienė) came to Lithuania, hoping — as the 70th anniversary of the liquidation of the Kaunas ghetto was being marked — to memorialize her late husband, Juda (Jehuda) Zupavičius, and his comrades.

"I am in our courtyard again. It's quiet. Many, many years have passed. I see myself, always hurrying, a young schoolgirl full of strength and energy. And later, happy with Juda, stepping into a new life. Then, one sunny Sunday morning, it all vanished like a beautiful dream..." — Dita Šperling (Zupavičienė)

The family photograph of Dita and Juda Zupavičius was transformed into a shattered fragment of mirror; beneath the image you'll find a short text, written in Dita's own hand, introducing this story and the marks of an unforgotten tragedy.

"Here, from 1939 to 1941, lived Dita and Juda Zupavičius. Juda was one of the leaders of the underground resistance in the Kaunas ghetto; brutally tortured by the Nazis, he never gave up the hiding places of the ghetto's children. Dita was his comrade in the struggle."

Dita Kacaitė met Juda Zupavičius in Kaunas in 1938. They married in February 1941 and settled together on Ožeškienės Street, in a house belonging to the Grinbergas family.

Jehuda Zupavičius was originally from Jonava and moved to Kaunas in the 1930s; he was a reserve officer in the Lithuanian army. While studying, he became friends with Zvi Kadushin. When all the Jews of Kaunas were moved into the ghetto, Zvi Kadushin — with the active support of deputy chief of the Jewish police Jehuda Zupavičius — began secretly photographing everyday life in the Kaunas ghetto. These photographs have become invaluable, and are shown in some of the world's most prominent Holocaust museums.

Among Zvi Kadushin's unforgettable photographs is this one too — taken a month before the fateful Children's Action and Juda's arrest, showing his friends Dita and Juda Zupavičius in the ghetto, in their small room.

In 2013, Dita Šperling (Zupavičienė) — Juda's comrade in the resistance — was awarded the Life Saving Cross for saving Rinutė Zupavičiūtė.

title: Portrait of the Mime
description:
The stories behind the gallery's other paintings are connected to former residents of the courtyard too. On the wall leading into the inner courtyard, a Mime with an old-fashioned camera was painted in 2013. The Mime doesn't just tell stories — he also blesses those coming and going.

If you look around, you'll find still more stories that we tell on the courtyard's inner and outer walls: photographs, knitwork, paintings, and other pieces invite you to reflect. These aren't just artworks made on a wall for their own sake — they always carry meaning, and they aim to change something, in the heart of the observer and in the surroundings as a whole.

title: Portrait of Leo Tolstoy
description: "While living among people, don't forget what you learned in solitude. And in solitude, reflect on what you learned while among people." — Leo Tolstoy

title: Portrait of Tomas and Jonas
description: The portrait of Tomas and Jonas was created at the request of neighbor Tomas, based on a blurry old photograph their mother loved dearly. Tomas told us that he and his brother Jonas are still the best of friends to this day, which is why this piece symbolizes friendship.

title: Neighbor Nijolė's Aunt
description: Neighbor Nijolė, the first to passionately support this idea, suggested painting a portrait of her aunt from an old photograph taken in 1936. Her aunt used to dress elegantly but was very strict. Nijolė remembered not only the wish to look beautiful, but also the fear she felt around her aunt — so I decided to honor her memory.

title: Portrait of Neighbor Bronė
description: Neighbor Regina lent us a photograph of her mother-in-law, Bronė — by all accounts, a tolerant, good-natured woman who witnessed the Jewish residents of this courtyard being driven from their homes when the war began. Lithuanian white-armband collaborators also worked alongside the Germans in this — something her mother-in-law suffered deeply over, though there was nothing she could do to help...

title: Tree of Life
description:
Mirrors — they're magical, as unpredictable as time and our own souls. The Lithuanian tree of life, symbols of family and love, alongside the mirrored flowers given as a gift to neighbor Nijolė — these pieces were like a new kind of play for artist Nomeda Balasevičiūtė, a book illustrator who once published the first newspaper for girls in Lithuania, later published children's books, and then, quite unexpectedly, turned to translation and tour-guiding — a path that led her to this courtyard, one her heart can no longer leave.

"Tree of Life." Artist Nomeda Balasevičiūtė. Kiemo Galerija, 2022.

title: Old Information Board
description:
The old information board with a changed address: (Stalino Ave. / Ožeškienės St. No. 15)

This considerably rusted board hung empty; our names appeared on it after a neighbors' dinner.

During the interwar period, once the Stalinist regime began, boards like this could be found in almost every larger courtyard.

These information boards were put up to make it easier to find residents who were being sought. During the repressions, the aim was to quickly locate people who were to be arrested and deported. But as people began disappearing more and more often, these boards stopped helping and instead started exposing the regime's own activity. So the boards were banned and ordered taken down — but here, in our courtyard, this one survived.

title: First Room
description:
In "The Room of Bella, Sveta, and Saulė," seeing, knowing, and believing create a meaningful unity. The piece depicts three different women and a small girl, Una. Each of them, in her own way, constructs the mysterious perspective of her own life.

Sveta has already passed away, so this painting also serves to preserve her memory. Every day, she cared for her disabled husband — bringing him food, looking after him... Neighbor Sveta, carrying her bags, metaphorically questions the many-layered notion of feminine charm and self-sacrifice for others...

"The parents of artist Bella Shirin (before emigrating, Šifrisaitė) miraculously survived the Dachau concentration camp. After the war they settled in Kaunas, where their daughter Bella was born and raised. At seventeen, she emigrated to Israel with her parents (1963), though she always longed deeply for her homeland. She first returned to Kaunas in 2004, and in 2016 came back for good — and finally felt happy again. Today the artist lives near the street of her childhood, at Kiemo Galerija, and works every day toward bringing Jews and Lithuanians closer together..."

In the photograph, on the wall of the oldest still-operating watch repair shop, you can see the painting "Portrait of the Watchmaker's Family," depicting Bella Shirin's parents (by Vytenis Jakas, 2015).

Bella is a 2022 CEC ambassador, and her life story, her work, and her positive outlook on life inspire many of us to enjoy each day, even the small things, as a gift. Her figure is depicted standing on a black, shadow-like ball — a slightly modified rendering of the symbol that became associated with CEC. Standing on the ball, spinning a hoop around her waist, Bella dances the dance of her life. Her raised arms help her balance, her gaze always turned upward, toward the sky — after all, not even an angel perches on slumped shoulders. Let's always look boldly ahead, and when we're sad, when it hurts — let's sing, let's dance, and the sadness will pass...

Next to Saulė, wrapped in a cloak of stars and looking upward, is depicted the girl Una. The interplay between the smiling figures of Saulė and Una, gazing at something secret and visible only to the two of them, creates a mystery of its own — Saulė reaches out her hand to a small bird, carefully held behind her by the shadow of an older woman...

All the figures in the room are connected by a shared motif of shadow, echoing the profile of the "Venus de Milo" depicted at the center of the piece. As a symbol of beauty, Venus has probably always been, and remains, an aspiration toward physical perfection in women — armless, it's true...

So each of us is perfect and beautiful in our own way, each of us connected, in our own way, to one shared existence, to womanhood, tenderness, love, and the pursuit of an ideal of beauty.

title: Second Room
description:
In this room we see neighbor Albertas, Reginutė's husband — the two of them were like the courtyard's own good samaritans, always there together, whether someone needed help, or there was a celebration, or work to be done, or just someone to talk to — always ready to listen, to encourage, to quietly keep someone company... Sadly, after an unexpected diagnosis of a severe, incurable illness, Albertas left us — just as quietly, as if apologizing... And yet this man's simple, everyday presence, marked by gentleness and acceptance of things as they were, left a scar of longing in the hearts of those who knew him. Now his shadow touches the ball of thread that chases after eternity, connecting all of us — and the figures in these rooms too...

The young man playing accordion, Tomas, was once a frequent guest at Kiemo Galerija — a cheerful musician who now lives in the United Kingdom. Tomas sits on an antique column, his gaze turned away from the viewer, off to the side, as if lost in the music.

The figure turned toward the viewer, staring at some point on the wall, is Vytenis — the author of the "Rooms of Secrets" painting. (Portrait by artist Jūratė Kluonė, 2015.)

title: Third Room
description:
The secret enveloping Julija's room is probably best left for each viewer to decide for themselves — everyone will see this room's occupant a little differently... Julija's neighbors know she's a dancer and that she adores her cat, Mažylis... The various characters she's played on the dance stage aren't the real Julija, and yet they leave meaningful impressions, both in her own heart and in the hearts of those watching her. At moments it can feel hard to tell shadow from reality, role from self... everything turns in a circle, life becomes an arena where each of us plays the part we've been given, where we're all constantly in motion, as in this room... only the artist's brush, as if by chance, has briefly frozen a moment in which, against the backdrop of a gracefully dancing cat's shadow, an elegantly alluring young woman sits leaning back a little.

Her gaze is turned toward the wall, where a mirror hangs — a housewarming gift from her parents. Do mirrors really reflect reality? Or are we ourselves the mirrors, endlessly reflecting light, shadow, hope, dreams...

title: Fourth Room
description:
In Dita's room we travel back to the 1920s, when a young Jewish girl, Dita, in love with her neighbor Ika, would turn off the light in the evenings and look toward the window of the building across from hers, hoping to catch a glimpse of him... Past and present, the Holocaust that forever separated these eternal sweethearts, and a love stronger than death... Facing the oval mirrored window on the opposite wall is an elegant woman of advanced years — the same girl Dita, become in this piece the shadow of her older self. The mirrored window reflects the present, yet within it lie shadows of the past too — recurring events, places, streams of thought. Two chapters of a woman's life, youth and old age, symbolized by the wheel and the walking stick Dita holds. On the room's walls, a great many empty frames seem to ask a question — do we hold on to every memory, will we find, coming back full circle, what we once left behind, do we return the same people we were...

The frames mark the boundaries of the painting, yet the piece also questions the idea of freeing oneself from the past, from memories, from attachments — and at the same time raises a question about the hope of eternal life. We are like the frames of a painting, holding a spiritual world invisible to outsiders; we paint the pictures of our own lives, visible only to us, which perhaps will only find their meaning in the light of eternity. In this room, the shadow of a girl gazing at a mirrored window reflecting the clouds and sky guards the secret of her love, still unaware of so much to come... Looking toward that same window, the eyes of a woman nearly a hundred years old see the very same clouds and sky, while a clock behind her counts out the minutes and seconds... After nearly a hundred years, Dita's room became the windows of neighbor Saulė's apartment... Though Dita and Saulė never met, love, faith, and hope remain the secret shared by the windows of their homes...

title: Fifth Room
description:
1941. The new residents of the courtyard — Romas's parents.

Neighbor Romas stands in the room naked, wrapped in a towel, carrying a bucket. Neighbors know Romas was one of the oldest residents of the courtyard. His apartment didn't always have running water — in the old days it had to be carried up to the third floor in buckets... Romas's life was full of contradiction — at one point he was the building's caretaker; later he couldn't resist the pull of vodka, and for many years he could no longer walk and lay in bed... A few years ago he even started a fire, having fallen asleep in bed with a cigarette... firefighters rushed over — not with buckets, admittedly — to put out the fire Romas had caused...

To the neighbors who visited him in his final years, Romas liked to tell stories of the love affairs of his youth... When we leave this world we take nothing with us, and we can bring nothing more — we can't apologize, we can't be different... Romas is gone now, but the memories of him prompt us to reflect on whose burdens we carry, and what kind, and on the fact that, in the end, none of us takes anything with us...

title: Sixth Room
description:
This room depicts five renowned artists from around the world, arriving from different eras and countries into this room belonging to sculptor Grybas, together discussing a unique episode in the history of Kaunas: have you ever heard of a city putting up the wrong monument? In Kaunas, in 1990, exactly that happened — we put up the wrong monument to Vytautas the Great!

As part of Kiemo Galerija's "Rooms of Secrets" mural cycle, artist Vytenis Jakas created the mural "Relics of the Battle of Grunwald" in 2022 — a visual reconstruction of a fragment of a letter by V. Grybas. The monument to Vytautas the Great is recreated in the mural authentically, exactly as sculptor Vincas Grybas originally designed it, undistorted, in 1930. The sculptor is depicted in the mural blindfolded — because it is we, across various periods of history, who blindfolded him, wanting him to believe in something he himself never created. His blindfolded eyes also symbolize us: unwilling, even with our eyes wide open, to look honestly at our own history. Written across the sculpture's pedestal is the inscription DADA IS EVERYWHERE, recalling the marks left on city walls — by street kids and genuine artists alike — and tying the monument to the modernist artistic spirit of both Western Europe and the wider world. Falling across the sculptor's blindfolded eyes is the shadow of the Vytautas the Great sculpture that now stands on Laisvės Alėja, together with the shadow of the allied soldiers' bas-reliefs that were, as discussed earlier, imposed onto it.

And yet, the problem isn't the monument itself — the problem is where it was put on display, and the fact that, in our haste, without properly examining the prewar context or sculptor V. Grybas's original proposal, in 1990 we put up entirely the wrong monument to Vytautas the Great.

This monument, supposedly dedicated to commemorating the Battle of Grunwald, actually contradicts historical truth. To this day, accounts of who actually won the Battle of Grunwald don't agree...

Take notice of the four nations kneeling beneath Vytautas's feet. Were they obedient servants of his power — or were they allies? Which means there's another story hidden here, one we didn't know when the monument was re-erected in 1990...

Why was the monument to Vytautas put up on a military base in 1932, rather than on Laisvės Alėja?

These and other questions can only be answered by looking more closely at the prewar context. Here I'll just briefly mention that the monument to Vytautas the Great re-erected in 1990 is a copy of a monument commissioned by the Lithuania Military Academy that once stood in Aukštoji Panemunė (later demolished). Before the war, the monument to Vytautas the Great created by sculptor V. Grybas was meant to educate and inspire soldiers for an important campaign — the liberation of Vilnius. This interwar monument was intended to prepare soldiers for battle according to a particular military doctrine, and was built to stand within a closed military area — in other words, it was never meant to be displayed publicly.

Let that sculpture remain in the shadow of our history, while the sun, falling on the main monument at the right time of day, illuminates its path toward truth and its connection with both contemporary art and the spirit of our own time.

Visiting Vytenis's room, Dalí, Magritte, and Duchamp bring their own secrets along with them. On the left side of the room, a guest settled into a strange, though presumably comfortable, pose carries secrets of his own too... a cloud, a shadow standing behind him... This is Marcel Duchamp, one of the most influential artists of the 20th century and, incidentally, a passionate chess player, curiously examining this singular episode in the history of Kaunas. Since he couldn't stand artists and museums, art dealers and exhibition openings, he would most likely look favorably on the setting of Kiemo Galerija, saying during his visit his famous line: "A work of art does not exist on its own — it is those who look at it who create the work."
